summary: DHH argues that software development managers often use vague and ambiguous task assignments as a shield against accountability. When projects go wrong, the lack of concrete requirements gives managers an easy out to blame developers for misinterpreting instructions. DHH advises developers to fight back by insisting on detailed requirements documents written in plain language, and to get genuine sign-off from managers. He acknowledges this is difficult and will face resistance from managers comfortable with the status quo.
key_points: Vague assignments are a deliberate management tactic that shifts blame to developers when implementations don't match unstated expectations
Personal interpretations of ambiguous requirements will inevitably diverge between developer and manager, creating conflict
The countermeasure is a detailed requirements document written in plain language with genuine manager sign-off
Skilled practitioners of vague assignment can create a false sense of security that everything is understood when it isn't
money_quotes: Many software development managers like to avoid talking details when handing out assignments. It's the perfect defense against responsibility.
Escaping the concrete leaves an open window to claim that a given implementation is "all wrong and not at all what we agreed on".

The trick is to not to accept ambiguous and vague defined projects, because you will be guaranteed trouble if your personal interpretations of how the work should be done goes out of sync with that of your manager.
When executed by a seasoned practitioner, the art of ambiguous and vague task assignment can easily spellbind the best into believing that all is good, when it's not.
Getting a sign off on an unread document is worthless.
Closing the window on ambiguity and vagueness in assignments won't happen quickly or without a lot hard work.
key_insight: Vague task assignments are not laziness but a management defense mechanism against accountability, and developers must proactively demand concrete requirements to protect themselves.
